column1:
Which of the following is an abnormal flow change caused by congestive heart failure?

A: monophasic portal flow
B: increased velocities in the bilateral common carotid arteries
C: pulsatile flow in the proximal hepatic veins
D: decreased ankle pressures
column2:
Answer: D. decreased ankle pressures

Feedback:
CHF reduces cardiac output that leads to biphasic portal vein flow, decreased velocities in the bilateral common carotid arteries,
and reduced ankle pressures. The hepatic veins normally demonstrate pulsatile or triphasic flow due to the ripple effect from
cardiac motion. This pulsatility increases with CHF.

column1:
When evaluating a focal stenosis of the femoral artery, where should you place the cursor to obtain the highest velocity related to the stenosis?

A: immediately proximal to the focal point of the stenosis
B: 1-2cm distal to the focal point of the stenosis
C: at the narrowest point in the vessel or immediately distal to the focal point of the stenosis
D: 1-2cm proximal to the focal point of the stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. at the narrowest point in the vessel or immediately distal to the focal point of the stenosis

Feedback:
When evaluating a focal stenosis of an artery, you should place the cursor at the narrowest point in the vessel or immediately
distal to the focal point of the stenosis to obtain the highest velocity related to the stenosis. It is important to move the Doppler
cursor through the stenotic area and out the other side to locate the peak velocity.

column1:
A patient complains of right leg pain and color changes in the leg. When he lies down, the right calf and foot become pale. When he stands up, the right calf and foot become reddened. The left leg is asymptomatic. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: right popliteal stenosis
B: deep venous reflux
C: significant aortic obstruction
D: significant right iliac stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. right popliteal stenosis

Feedback:
Pallor and dependent rubor are indications of significant arterial disease. The unilateral presentation indicates the obstruction
is blocking flow to just the right leg. Only the lower leg experiences the color change, so the blockage must be at the level of
knee = right popliteal stenosis is the most likely cause

column1:
Why does blood move into smaller collateral vessels when a larger vessel is occluded?

A: the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is lower than the resistance in the occluded vessel
B: because of the significant drop in venous pressure
C: the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is higher than the resistance in the occluded vessel
D: to compensate for loss of blood flow proximal to the obstruction
column2:
Answer: A. the resistance to flow in the smaller collateral is lower than the resistance in the occluded vessel

Feedback:
Collateral flow around an occlusion develops to compensate for the reduction in flow to the distal tissues. When the native
artery is patent, the resistance to flow is greater in the smaller arteries. When the larger artery is occluded, the resistance to
flow in the smaller collateral is lower than the resistance in the occluded vessel. Blood flow moves into the vessel with the least
resistance.

column1:
You are performing a lower extremity arterial Doppler exam due to the increasing bilateral calf pain over the last few months. The images are taken from the proximal left and right external iliac arteries. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: AV malformation at the right groin
B: Leriche syndrome
C: May-Thurner syndrome
D: Paget-Schroetter syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. Leriche syndrome

Feedback:
Leriche syndrome refers to obstruction of the distal aorta. If both iliac arteries demonstrate post-stenotic flow patterns, there is
an obstruction proximally in the aorta.

column1:
Which of the following describes the abnormal response to papaverine injection during a penile impotence exam?

A: Decreased dorsal venous outflow
B: the PSV and EDV will increase post injection but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged
C: the PSV and EDV of the arterial inflow will decrease
D: increased reflux in the veins in the scrotal varicocele
column2:
Answer: C. the PSV and EDV of the arterial inflow will decrease

Feedback:
Doppler evaluation includes assessment of the cavernous arteries with Doppler pre- and post- medication injection (papaverine
most common); normally the PSV and EDV will increase post injection but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged.

column1:
This patient would benefit the most from what type of exam?

A: Arterial duplex exam
B: Venous duplex exam
C: Aortic duplex exam
D: Renal artery duplex exam
column2:
Answer: A. Arterial duplex exam

Feedback:
The image demonstrates an ulceration on the foot that is dry, crusty, deep and with regular borders, which is consistent with an
ulcer caused by arterial ischemia. An arterial duplex exam will evaluate the arterial system of the leg for areas of stenosis or
occlusion and allow the surgeon to consider the best type of treatment (bypass or amputation).

column1:
What critical finding is associated with calf paresthesia, localized muscle weakness, pain when stretching calf muscles, and drop foot?

A: post-phlebitic syndrome
B: peripheral arterial disease
C: anterior compartment syndrome
D: popliteal entrapment
column2:
Answer: C. anterior compartment syndrome

Feedback:
Acute compartment syndrome is a medical emergency. It is usually caused by a severe injury and without intervention, it can
lead to permanent muscle damage. Symptoms of compartment syndrome include paresthesia, localized muscle weakness, pain
when stretching calf muscles, drop foot.

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity arterial exam with mild bilateral leg pain. The 2D evaluation demonstrates minimal diffuse atherosclerosis bilaterally. The Doppler waveforms from the arteries of both legs demonstrate biphasic waveforms with increased acceleration time. Which of the following could explain these findings?

A: bilateral popliteal entrapment
B: cardiac ejection fraction 30%
C: cardiac ejection fraction 85%
D: systemic hypertension
column2:
Answer: B. cardiac ejection fraction 30%

Feedback:
Bilateral decrease in lower extremity velocities can indicate a cardiac issue, especially in the absence of aortic or bilateral iliac
disease. An increased AT means lower resistance flow with a slower upslope. The conversion of a normal triphasic waveform to
a biphasic waveform also indicates lower resistance flow.
Popliteal entrapment syndrome is a functional issue where the artery becomes compressed with contraction of the calf
muscles, usually seen in runners. Symptoms of this disorder would be related to activity and would not normally produce
constant mild pain. An exercise arterial exam would be necessary to diagnose this issue.
Systemic HTN and increased EF% would cause a shorter (decreased) AT and the flow would remain high resistance with elevated
velocities.

column1:
The common femoral artery velocity is 0.8 m/s and the proximal femoral artery demonstrates atherosclerosis formation causing an increase in the velocity to 3.2 m/s. This indicates what level of stenosis?

A: 50%
B: 25%
C: 75%
D: 100%
column2:
Answer: C. 75%

Feedback:
When the velocity between two extremity arterial segments doubles, this indicates at least a 50% stenosis. When the velocity
between two extremity arterial segments quadruples, this indicates at least a 75% stenosis.

column1:
You receive an order for an upper extremity arterial exam to rule out Buerger disease. What is the preferred method of evaluation?

A: Evaluate the smaller arteries of the arm with Duplex and PPG techniques
B: Evaluate the arteries of the shoulder for extrinsic compression with movement
C: Evaluate the arterial flow at rest and with exercise
D: Evaluate the hands for vascular changes with cold sensitivity testing
column2:
Answer: A. Evaluate the smaller arteries of the arm with Duplex and PPG techniques

Feedback:
Buerger disease refers to obstruction of the smaller arteries in the hands and feet. It is more common in males and smokers.
Because it tends to affect smaller vessels, PPG techniques are an important part of the exam to assess digital flow. Monophasic,
damped waveforms seen in the distal calf/arm vessels. Corkscrew collateral formation is a sign of this disorder - significantly
tortuous collateral formation.
Original Video - https://youtu.be/UvkQ9zwmUBo?si=hieYZrJgdV6g9yg4

column1:
What is the most common cardiac disorder associated with arterial embolism?

A: cardiomyopathy
B: Marfan syndrome
C: myocardial infarction
D: atrial fibrillation
column2:
Answer: D. atrial fibrillation

Feedback:
80-90% of arterial emboli are from a cardiac source. Atrial fibrillation can lead to clot formation in the left atrium. If the clot
breaks off, it can travel anywhere in the systemic arterial system. If the foramen ovale is patent, the clot could also travel into
the right heart and the pulmonary arteries.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes thoracic outlet syndrome?

A: PPG or PVR tracings can be used to evaluate a patient with suspected TOS
B: Patients commonly experiencing dull, aching pain and paresthesia in the affected knee and ankle
C: The Homan sign indicates a positive test result for TOS.
D: The Tinel test is used during assessment for TOS.
column2:
Answer: A. PPG or PVR tracings can be used to evaluate a patient with suspected TOS

Feedback:
Patients with TOS commonly experiencing dull, aching pain and paresthesia in the affected shoulder/arm. PPG or PVR tracings
can be used to evaluate a patient with suspected TOS using specific maneuvers during testing. The Homan sign refers to pain in
the calf with quick dorsiflexion of the foot, seen with DVT. The Tinel sign indicates a damaged nerve at the wrist and is
associated with hypothenar hammer syndrome.

column1:
An abnormality in which of the following vessels could be the cause of vasculogenic impotence?

A: internal and external iliac arteries
B: common and internal iliac arteries
C: external iliac and femoral arteries
D: aorta and gonadal arteries
column2:
Answer: B. common and internal iliac arteries

Feedback:
The external iliac artery supplies blood to the leg. The internal iliac artery supplies blood to the branches that supply the penis.
If there are flow abnormalities in the aorta, internal iliac artery or common iliac artery, this can lead to vasculogenic impotence.
The gonadal arteries originate from the aorta and supply blood to the scrotum and testicles.

column1:
Which of the following has no effect on the severity of an arterial stenosis?

A: polycythemia vera
B: peripheral resistance
C: vessel radius
D: valvular competence
column2:
Answer: D. valvular competence

Feedback:
The veins contain valves, not the arteries. Valvular incompetence will not affect arterial stenosis.

column1:
An AV malformation is most commonly caused by:

A: hemodialysis access
B: recent angiography procedure
C: a congenital defect
D: trauma
column2:
Answer: C. a congenital defect

Feedback:
An AVM is a congenital defect. An AVF is caused by trauma or an interventional procedure.

column1:
Paresthesia is defined as:

A: an abnormal skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling
B: loss of vision in one eye for a short period of time
C: dizziness related to change in patient position
D: loss of ability to move an extremity or other body part
column2:
Answer: A. an abnormal skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling

Feedback:
Paralysis is defined as loss of ability to move an extremity or other body part. Paresthesia refers to an abnormal skin sensation,
such as numbness or tingling.

column1:
A 70% stenosis in the proximal femoral artery will cause the __________________ in the mid femoral artery.

A: acceleration time to decrease
B: velocity to increase
C: Reynold's number to decrease
D: acceleration time to increase
column2:
Answer: D. acceleration time to increase

Feedback:
Acceleration time refers to the time from the start of systole to peak systole. High resistance vessels have short acceleration
times. Low resistance vessels and areas distal to a stenosis will demonstrate longer acceleration times.

column1:
The Scimitar sign is associated with:

A: Bovine arch
B: Adventitial cysts
C: Median arcuate ligament syndrome
D: Femoral to Femoral bypass graft
column2:
Answer: B. Adventitial cysts

Feedback:
The Scimitar sign is associated with adventitial cyst formation. The arterial lumen is compressed by the cyst and is said to look
like a scimitar (type of curved sword).

column1:
Adventitial cysts:

A: most commonly affect the popliteal artery
B: are usually treated with bypass graft placement
C: are a normal variant that can be seen in up to 10% of patients
D: are more common in women
column2:
Answer: A. most commonly affect the popliteal artery

Feedback:
Adventitial cysts are a rare condition that involves the collection of mucinous material within the adventitial wall layer of the
affected vessel. They predominantly affect the peripheral arteries with the popliteal artery most commonly affected. The cysts
are most common in young to middle-aged men. The can be treated by ultrasound guided cyst aspiration.

column1:
Patients with this risk factor have a greater incidence of atherosclerotic disease in walls of the vessels in the calf.

A: Diabetes
B: Raynaud syndrome
C: Post-phlebitic syndrome
D: Systemic HTN
column2:
Answer: A. Diabetes

Feedback:
Diabetic patients suffer from medial calcification of the blood vessel walls. This leads to increased resistance in the vessels and
decreased flow.

column1:
If there is a 75% stenosis of the distal axillary artery, which of the following arteries will demonstrate a triphasic waveform?

A: subclavian
B: radial
C: ulnar
D: brachial
column2:
Answer: A. subclavian

Feedback:
A 75% stenosis in the axillary artery will cause a significant loss of flow distally. The resistance in the distal vessels will drop and
the waveforms will become monophasic. The subclavian artery is proximal to the stenosis and will remain high resistance.

column1:
Which of the following is used to determine the capability of healing a wound or identify a site for amputation?

A: Ascending venography
B: Transcutaneous oximetry
C: Sclerotherapy
D: Photoplethysmography
column2:
Answer: B. Transcutaneous oximetry

Feedback:
Transcutaneous oximetry evaluates oxygen supply and consumption in the tissues surrounding a wound. The partial pressure
of the oxygen is recorded in the tissues around the wound. 70-80mmHg indicates normal pressure, <40mmHg indicates tissue
hypoxia. The information is used to determine the healing ability of the wound.

column1:
The profunda artery demonstrates a 75% stenosis. Which of the following arteries will demonstrate monophasic flow due to the stenosis?

A: popliteal
B: posterior tibial
C: anterior tibial
D: none of the above
column2:
Answer: D. none of the above

Feedback:
The femoral artery supplies the popliteal and calf vessels with blood. A stenosis in the profunda artery will not cause decreased
resistance in the vessels of the lower leg.

column1:
A velocity is recorded in the distal femoral artery of 2.75m/s, what hemodynamic changes will be seen in popliteal artery?

A: high velocity, peaked waveform
B: increased diastolic flow reversal
C: decreased acceleration time
D: increased acceleration time
column2:
Answer: D. increased acceleration time

Feedback:
The resistance distal to the stenosis will be decreased and flow will become monophasic. Acceleration time is prolonged with
lower resistance flow.

column1:
If a patient suffers from ischemic rest pain in the feet, what maneuver can relieve the pain?

A: Standing
B: Sitting with legs elevated
C: Walking
D: Valsalva maneuver
column2:
Answer: A. Standing

Feedback:
Ischemic rest pain is described as burning pain in the ball of the foot and toes that is worse at night when the patient is in bed.
Standing STILL or hanging the legs in a dependent position would use gravity to assist the arterial flow in entering the feet at a
faster rate. Walking and elevating the legs would continue to increase the need for arterial inflow and exacerbate the
symptoms.

column1:
Which of the following measurements is used to differentiate inflow from outflow disease in the lower extremity arteries?

A: Pulsatility index
B: Acceleration time
C: S/D ratio
D: Resistive index
column2:
Answer: B. Acceleration time

Feedback:
Acceleration Time is measured to differentiate inflow from outflow disease in the lower extremity. Measure the time from the
onset of systole to the peak of systole on the waveform.
>140ms indicates inflow disease. If the AT in the common femoral artery is >140ms, iliac disease is suspected. If the AT in both
of the common femoral arteries is >140ms, aortic disease is suspected.
Shorter AT is associated with distal obstruction. With a distal obstruction, the systolic upstroke will become steeper = shorter
time from onset to peak.

column1:
Jaw claudication, headaches and neck pain, and asymmetric brachial blood pressures are all symptoms related to:

A: thoracic outlet syndrome
B: Marfan syndrome
C: thromboangiitis obliterans
D: giant cell arteritis
column2:
Answer: D. giant cell arteritis

Feedback:
Giant Cell Arteritis: Temporal arteritis is associated with headaches and tenderness over the artery. Asymmetric brachial blood
pressures are seen in patients with arteritis in extremities. Other symptoms include pain and/or stiffness in the neck, jaw
claudication, and visual disturbances.
TOS causes upper extremity symptoms. Thromboangiitis obliterans causes lower leg/feet/forearm/hand symptoms. Marfan
syndrome is a connective tissue disorder associated with aortic dissection,

column1:
A patient presents with a history of right hip and thigh pain with numbness when he walks. The exercise time varies before the pain starts and it is relieved by sitting down. There are no symptoms in his lower leg. These findings are most suggestive of?

A: peripheral arterial disease
B: chronic venous disease
C: Raynaud's disease
D: neurogenic cause
column2:
Answer: D. neurogenic cause

Feedback:
The leg pain is not consistent and reproducible which are key components of claudication related to vascular disease.
Neurogenic claudication is related to nerve compression proximal to the symptoms. The feeling of pain and paresthesia is
similar to that with arterial disease, but the exercise time varies before the symptoms occur. The symptoms experienced with
arterial disease are in the distal leg, while symptoms confined to the upper leg are typically related to nerve compression.

column1:
Which of the following is considered a risk factor for peripheral arterial disease?

A: Diabetes
B: History of deep vein thrombosis
C: Pregnancy
D: Excessive, repetitive standing
column2:
Answer: A. Diabetes

Feedback:
Diabetes will expedite hardening of the arterial walls, especially in smaller arteries. The calf and digital arteries are first affected
by medial calcification. Pregnancy and excessive standing can lead to an increase in venous blood volume and stasis, but has no
effect on the formation of arterial disease. A history of DVT does not affect the risk of atherosclerotic disease.

column1:
A patient presents with leg pain 2 days after a coronary artery catheterization. All of the following are an important part of the physical evaluation of the patient, except:

A: Evaluation of the lower leg for loss of hair, dry skin and thick toenails
B: Evaluation of the toes for blue discoloration
C: Evaluation of the leg for signs of pallor
D: Evaluation of the groin for a bruit
column2:
Answer: A. Evaluation of the lower leg for loss of hair, dry skin and thick toenails

Feedback:
Loss of hair, dry skin and thick toenails are symptoms of chronic disease that would not really be important to the acute onset
of the new symptoms. Angiography procedures have a risk of related pseudoaneurysm, AV fistula (bruit), distal embolization
(blue toes) and acute occlusion from the puncture.

column1:
Thromboangiitis obliterans involves inflammation of:

A: all vessel walls with no effect on connective tissue
B: intimal wall layer
C: all vessel walls and surrounding connective tissue
D: intimal and media wall
column2:
Answer: C. all vessel walls and surrounding connective tissue

Feedback:
Thromboangiitis refers to diffuse inflammation of the intimal wall layer. Thromboangiitis obliterans refers to inflammation of all
vessel walls and surrounding connective tissue. Buerger disease is a type of thromboangiitis obliterans.

column1:
If the acceleration time is >140ms in both common femoral arteries:

A: unilateral external iliac disease is suspected
B: flow is considered normal.
C: aortic disease is suspected
D: bilateral internal iliac disease is suspected
column2:
Answer: C. aortic disease is suspected

Feedback:
If the acceleration time is >140ms in both common femoral arteries, aortic disease is suspected. Increased or longer AT
indicates a proximal obstruction.
If the acceleration time is >140ms in one common femoral artery, ipsilateral iliac stenosis is suspected. Decreased or shorter AT
indicates a distal obstruction.

column1:
Patients with lower extremity arterial disease may describe pain

A: that is relieved by walking
B: that is relieved by elevating the legs
C: that is relieved by placing legs in a dependent position
D: that is relieved by lying down
column2:
Answer: C. that is relieved by placing legs in a dependent position

Feedback:
The dependent position helps blood to reach the lower legs more quickly. Walking and elevating the legs would increase the
pain. Lying down would provide minimal relief due to the loss of gravity's assistance with the flow.

column1:
The most common color change demonstrated in a lower extremity with an acute arterial occlusion is:

A: brawny
B: pallor
C: cyanosis
D: rubor
column2:
Answer: B. pallor

Feedback:
Six "P"s of Acute Arterial Occlusion:
1. Pain
2. Pallor
3. Pulselessness
4. Paresthesia
5. Paralysis
6. Polar Sensation (Cold)
***Caused by embolism, thrombus formation or trauma

column1:
A patient presents with groin pain 3 days after undergoing right femoral artery angioplasty. The image displayed is from the right groin in the area of pain. What should you report on your technologist worksheet?

A: An abnormally enlarged lymph node is present.
B: A pseudoaneurysm is present that is nearly thrombosed
C: A normal lymph node is present.
D: A pseudoaneurysm is present that requires immediate intervention
column2:
Answer: A. An abnormally enlarged lymph node is present.

Feedback:
Abnormal lymph nodes have a more rounded shape with hypoechoic/anechoic cortical tissue. The hilum will become less
visible. Posterior enhancement can also be noted due to the edema in the cortex.

column1:
If there is a left subclavian steal, which brachial artery evaluation will be abnormal?

A: Right brachial will have a higher BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform
B: Left brachial will have a lower BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform
C: Left brachial will have a higher BP and a triphasic waveform
D: Right brachial will have a lower BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform
column2:
Answer: B. Left brachial will have a lower BP and a blunted/monophasic waveform

Feedback:
If there is a left sided steal, this indicates the left subclavian artery has an occlusion at the origin. The left vertebral artery
originates from the left subclavian artery, just distal to its origin from the arch. The left vertebral artery now receives no
antegrade flow toward the head, due to the subclavian blockage.
Blood flows toward the head in the right vertebral artery to join the left vertebral artery to form the basilar artery. Some flow
from the right vertebral reverses into the left vertebral artery to feed the left subclavian artery and arm.
The BP in the left arm drops due to the blood flow coming in from the low resistance, cerebral circulation. The distal arm
pressure will decrease to allow flow to enter the "empty" vessels above, in a retrograde fashion.
Vertebral flow is low resistance and monophasic. The Doppler evaluation of the left arm will demonstrate these characteristics
which are abnormal for an extremity.

column1:
Which of the following is the most likely cause for the waveform displayed?

A: peripheral arterial disease
B: venous insufficiency
C: stenosis of the internal iliac artery
D: vasoconstriction in the pedal arteries
column2:
Answer: A. peripheral arterial disease

Feedback:
The image demonstrates an abnormal monophasic flow pattern in the PTA. Peripheral arterial disease can cause this abnormal
flow pattern. Venous insufficiency does not affect the arterial inflow. Vasoconstriction of the pedal arteries would increase the
resistance to flow in the calf arteries. Stenosis of the internal iliac arteries would cause ischemia to the pelvic organs, not the
extremity.

column1:
Arterial claudication occurs:

A: distal to the obstruction
B: while the patient is resting
C: while the legs are in the dependent position
D: proximal to the obstruction
column2:
Answer: A. distal to the obstruction

Feedback:
Arterial claudication occurs distal to the hemodynamically significant obstruction. The tissues distal to the obstruction are not
receiving the necessary oxygen which causes cramping and pain.

column1:
Which of the following patients would benefit from a Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty?

A: patient with left ICA occlusion
B: patient with recurrent pulmonary embolism
C: patient with 80% stenosis in proximal SFA
D: patient with chronic iliac DVT
column2:
Answer: C. patient with 80% stenosis in proximal SFA

Feedback:
Percutaneous Transluminal Angioplasty is used to open areas of stenosis in an artery. This intervention cannot be performed on
a fully occluded vessel.

column1:
An AV fistula is present connecting the femoral artery and vein at the right groin. The Doppler evaluation of the common femoral artery will demonstrate what flow pattern?

A: peaked, triphasic waveform
B: monophasic with minimal diastolic flow
C: biphasic flow with increased diastolic flow reversal
D: monophasic with increased diastolic flow
column2:
Answer: D. monophasic with increased diastolic flow

Feedback:
A traumatic AV fistula created during an invasive procedure or injury results in the same flow patterns as one created
intentionally for dialysis. The low resistance venous connection causes decreased resistance and monophasic flow in the inflow
artery proximal to the fistula.

column1:
__________________ uses a balloon tipped catheter to compress the atheroma and expand the vessel lumen.

A: An atherectomy procedure
B: An endarterectomy procedure
C: An angioplasty procedure
D: A valvulotome procedure
column2:
Answer: C. An angioplasty procedure

Feedback:
Atherectomy uses a catheter with a rotating head and suction capabilities to break up the plaque and remove it from the artery.
Angioplasty uses a balloon tipped catheter to compress the atheroma and expand the vessel lumen. An endarterectomy refers
to when the carotid artery is lacerated, and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal. A valvulotome procedure is used to
remove valves from inside a vein.

column1:
If the tibioperoneal trunk is occluded, flow in which calf vessel(s) will demonstrate increased flow?

A: anterior tibial artery
B: posterior tibial artery
C: peroneal artery
D: posterior tibial artery and peroneal artery
column2:
Answer: A. anterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The popliteal artery bifurcates into the anterior tibial artery and tibioperoneal trunk just outside the popliteal fossa. Slightly
distal to that bifurcation the trunk splits into the posterior tibial artery and peroneal artery. If the TP trunk is occluded, flow will
be redirected into the ATA. Compensatory flow will be directed to the ATA.

column1:
What structure compresses the artery in popliteal entrapment?

A: medial meniscus
B: medial head of the gastrocnemius muscle
C: lateral meniscus
D: patella
column2:
Answer: B. medial head of the gastrocnemius muscle

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment is the compression of the artery by the medial head of the gastrocnemius muscle. It is commonly found in
runners and athletes and is the most common cause of unilateral claudication in young patients.

column1:
Which of the following is the most common site for atherosclerosis formation in the lower extremity?

A: CFA bifurcation
B: SFA in Adductor canal
C: Popliteal artery
D: Calf trifurcation
column2:
Answer: B. SFA in Adductor canal

Feedback:
The most common site for atherosclerosis formation in the lower extremity is in the SFA in the adductor canal.

column1:
An acute occlusion occurs in the common iliac artery on the left side. Which of the following vessels will also have absent flow?

A: external iliac artery only
B: common femoral artery and saphenous artery
C: internal iliac artery and gonadal artery
D: common femoral, external iliac and internal iliac artery
column2:
Answer: D. common femoral, external iliac and internal iliac artery

Feedback:
The external iliac and internal iliac arteries are branches of the common iliac artery. The external iliac artery becomes the
common femoral artery once it crosses beneath the inguinal artery.

column1:
The images below are from a lower extremity arterial duplex examination. Review the information on the images and choose the waveform below that is most consistent with the expected waveform for the left popliteal artery. Image A Image B Image C Image D

A: Image D
B: Image A
C: Image B
D: Image C
column2:
Answer: B. Image A

Feedback:
The PTA waveform demonstrates monophasic, low resistance flow, but the ATA flow appears normal. This is most consistent
with stenosis in the tibioperoneal trunk or an isolated stenosis in the proximal PTA. If the peroneal artery flow is normal = PTA
stenosis. If the peroneal artery flow is abnormal = Tibioperoneal trunk stenosis. The popliteal artery should demonstrate a
normal, high resistance flow pattern because it is proximal to the area of stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following waveforms is most consistent with flow related to a pseudoaneurysm in the groin area?

A: A
B: D
C: C
D: B
column2:
Answer: A. A

Feedback:
Flow in the neck and body of a pseudoaneurysm is described as "to-and-fro". Waveform A is from a pseudoaneurysm in the
groin. Flow moves in and out of the blind ended pocket in the tissues. Waveform B is a normal triphasic waveform from a lower
extremity. Waveform C is a normal waveform from the renal parenchyma. Waveform D is taken from a vertebral artery and
demonstrates early systolic deceleration. It is associated with proximal stenosis and considered a pre-steal phenomenon.

column1:
Which of the following describes an AV malformation?

A: caused by trauma or surgical procedures
B: direct connection between a single artery and vein
C: can be treated using Thrombin injection
D: dilated venous collaterals adjacent to the area of interest
column2:
Answer: D. dilated venous collaterals adjacent to the area of interest

Feedback:
An AVM is a congenital malformation with multiple connections between the arterioles and venules without a capillary bed in
between. Venous collaterals will develop and dilate over time. An AVF is an acquired condition that is caused by trauma or
invasive procedures. Because it is an acute complication, no collateral vessels form. The arterial inflow and venous outflow will
demonstrate the same hemodynamic characteristics for an AVM and an AVF, low resistance arterial inflow and pulsatile,
turbulent venous outflow Thrombin cannot be used to treat an AVM or AVF because it could occlude the arterial system
turbulent venous outflow. Thrombin cannot be used to treat an AVM or AVF because it could occlude the arterial system.

column1:
A patient is referred for an arterial duplex exam for leg pain. The chart describes brown discoloration of the calves and prominent pedal pulses. Which of the following is an expected finding on today's exam?

A: popliteal aneurysm
B: normal arterial flow
C: Raynaud Syndrome
D: medial calcification
column2:
Answer: B. normal arterial flow

Feedback:
Brawny discoloration of the calf is usually a sign of venous disease. Prominent pedal pulses indicate strong arterial flow in the
tibial arteries. The patient will most likely demonstrate normal arterial flow.

column1:
If significant disease is identified on the arterial duplex evaluation, but 4 minutes of exercise has little effect on the ankle pressures:

A: there are collateral pathways present
B: post-occlusive reactive hyperemia should be performed
C: digital pressures must be recorded to diagnose disease in this patient
D: service should be scheduled for the ultrasound unit
column2:
Answer: A. there are collateral pathways present

Feedback:

column1:
A bilateral decrease in the femoral pulses can be related to:

A: aortic coarctation
B: DVT formation
C: occlusion of the deep femoral artery
D: occlusion of the popliteal artery
column2:
Answer: A. aortic coarctation

Feedback:
Aortic coarctation refers to the overgrowth of normal tissue in the aorta causing obstruction. It most commonly occurs in the
arch just distal to the origin of the left subclavian artery. Because the aorta is narrowed proximal to the iliac bifurcation, both
legs will demonstrate significantly lower pressures and abnormal Doppler waveforms.

column1:
Which of the following is the second most common site for atherosclerosis formation in the lower extremity?

A: common carotid artery bifurcation
B: popliteal trifurcation
C: distal SFA in Adductor canal
D: common femoral artery bifurcation
column2:
Answer: D. common femoral artery bifurcation

Feedback:
The distal SFA in the adductor canal is the most common site of atherosclerosis formation in the legs. The CFA bifurcation is the
second most common site of atherosclerosis formation in the legs.

column1:
Which of the following are collagen disorders that have increased risk for arterial dissection?

A: Shone complex and Blue Toe syndrome
B: Paget Schroetter syndrome and Raynaud syndrome
C: Marfan syndrome and Ehler-Danlos syndrome
D: Marfan syndrome and Polycythemia vera
column2:
Answer: C. Marfan syndrome and Ehler-Danlos syndrome

Feedback:
Marfan syndrome and Ehler-Danlos syndrome are collagen disorders that can lead to the formation of arterial aneurysm or
dissection. Shone complex is a group of cardiac defects and defects of the aortic arch and valve. Blue Toe syndrome is caused
by emboli from an AAA with mural thrombus, Paget Schroetter syndrome affects the upper extremity veins. Raynaud syndrome
is a collagen disorder that reduces flow to the hands/feet. Polycythemia vera is a rare cancer that causes blood thickening,

column1:
Which of the following describes the correct protocol for evaluating suspected popliteal entrapment?

A: Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the distal femoral artery at rest and with plantar extension
B: Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the popliteal artery at rest and with plantar flexion
C: Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the popliteal vein at rest and with plantar flexion
D: Evaluate the toe pressures at rest and during plantar extension
column2:
Answer: B. Use Doppler to evaluate the flow in the popliteal artery at rest and with plantar flexion

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment involves the intermittent compression of the popliteal artery with flexing of the calf muscle. Arterial inflow
is reduced causing pain in the calf muscle and foot due to ischemia. The velocity of the flow in the vessel is important to
evaluate the degree of stenosis that is caused by movement. PPG is the preferred method of evaluation, but Doppler evaluation
can also be used.

column1:
If the distal femoral artery demonstrates a peak velocity of 3.2m/s, the waveform in the posterior tibial artery will most likely be _____________.

A: monophasic
B: biphasic
C: absent
D: triphasic
column2:
Answer: A. monophasic

Feedback:
Normal flow in the PTA would be triphasic. A 3.2m/s PSV in the femoral artery indicates a hemodynamically significant stenosis
which would cause decreased resistance distally. The popliteal and calf arterial waveforms will most likely be monophasic.

column1:
Thrombolytic therapy is used to treat:

A: chronic venous disease
B: chronic deep vein thrombosis
C: acute arterial occlusion caused by thrombus
D: venous insufficiency
column2:
Answer: C. acute arterial occlusion caused by thrombus

Feedback:
Thrombolytic drugs are used in medicine to dissolve blood clots. Thrombolytic therapy is used to treat extensive acute DVT and
acute arterial obstruction by thrombus.

column1:
Which of the following can lead to an increase in the frequency shift detected in the common femoral artery?

A: 80% stenosis of the superficial femoral artery
B: Proximal superficial femoral artery occlusion
C: Decreased probe frequency
D: 80% stenosis of the common femoral artery
column2:
Answer: D. 80% stenosis of the common femoral artery

Feedback:
Stenosis in the CFA will increase the detected frequency shift (and calculated velocity). Using a higher frequency probe will
increase the detected frequency shift. Significant stenosis or occlusion of the SFA will reduce the detected frequency shift
(velocity) in the CFA.

column1:
Which of the following provides the greatest risk for peripheral arterial disease?

A: Excessive standing
B: Excessive sitting
C: Hyperlipidemia
D: Polycythemia vera
column2:
Answer: C. Hyperlipidemia

Feedback:
Hyperlipidemia refers to high cholesterol which is a risk factor for arterial disease. Excessive sitting or standing can lead to
venous stasis and thrombus formation. Polycythemia vera causes thickening of the blood which can lead to venous stasis and
thrombus formation.

column1:
A patient presents complaining of pain and discoloration in their hands when they are cold, as seen in the image. The expected characteristics of the digital PPG waveform in this patient after their hands have been submerged in 40 degree water for 1 minute would include:

A: Increased amplitude with a prominent dicrotic notch
B: Triphasic waveform
C: Monophasic damped waveform
D: Due to varied levels of vasoconstriction, flow will fluctuate between low resistance and high resistance until hand temperature returns to normal.
column2:
Answer: C. Monophasic damped waveform

Feedback:
The normal PPG waveform has a sharp peak with a downslope containing the dicrotic notch. In patients with Raynaud disease,
severe vasoconstriction occurs causing flow changes. As flow decreases, the dicrotic notch disappears and then the waveform
becomes lower and lower in amplitude.

column1:
A patient presents for a follow up exam on a lower extremity femoral- popliteal in-situ vein graft that was placed in the right leg 2 weeks ago. He is experiencing pain, pallor and paresthesia in the right foot. Which of the following is not an expected finding on the exam?

A: anastomosis narrowed by an aberrant suture
B: thrombosis around a valve remnant
C: extension of atherosclerotic plaque into the graft
D: graft kinking
column2:
Answer: C. extension of atherosclerotic plaque into the graft

Feedback:
Atherosclerosis is a chronic disease and would not progress into the area of the graft in such a short time period. The other
choices listed are all potential acute complications following graft placement.

column1:
Secondary Raynaud phenomenon:

A: occurs in patients with severe venous disease
B: is a functional vasospastic disorder which occurs in patients with an unobstructed vascular system
C: occurs in patients with an obstructed vascular system
D: is a functional vasospastic disorder that occurs due to kidney disease
column2:
Answer: C. occurs in patients with an obstructed vascular system

Feedback:
Primary - NL vascular system, intermittent bilateral digital arterial spasm, excellent prognosis; Raynaud syndrome or disease
Secondary - ABNL obstructed system with vasoconstriction causes constant ischemia, may be unilateral; Raynaud phenomenon

column1:
While scanning a patient following a recent car accident you identify an arteriovenous connection in the left groin. How can you determine if this finding is related to the recent trauma or it is a congenital abnormality?

A: Evaluate the deep veins for associated thrombus
B: Locate the number of connections between the artery and the vein
C: Perform a Doppler evaluation of flow within the connected vessels
D: Determine the location of the arteriovenous connection
column2:
Answer: B. Locate the number of connections between the artery and the vein

Feedback:
A congenital malformation (AVM) will usually have multiple connections between the artery and the vein. An AV fistula(AVF)
caused by trauma is usually a single connection between the artery and vein. The other items listed would be possible exam
techniques used to evaluate the patient, but they will not differentiate a congenital AVM from a traumatic AVF

column1:
When Thrombin is injected into a pseudoaneurysm, the needle puncture site should be:

A: within 1cm of the neck
B: on the lateral aspect of the groin
C: within 5mm of the neck
D: located as far from the neck as possible
column2:
Answer: D. located as far from the neck as possible

Feedback:
When injecting Thrombin, the physician inserts a 21-22g needle into the body of the pseudoaneurysm and confirms the location
with 2D ultrasound. Color Doppler should not be used during the procedure because it can obscure the needle position.
Thrombin must be injected at a location far from the neck of the pseudoaneurysm to prevent distribution into the arterial
system. 0.5 - 1.0mL injected in small increments and thrombosis is monitored on real time imaging. Thrombosis should begin
immediately (within 5-10 seconds). Once the flow in the pseudoaneurysm has been eliminated, color and Doppler are used to
confirm the absence of flow.

column1:
Congestive heart failure is more commonly seen with ____________________, while distal ischemia is more commonly seen with _________________.

A: distal AV fistula, proximal AV fistula
B: AV fistula, hemodialysis fistula
C: proximal AV fistula, distal AV fistula
D: AV malformation, AV fistula
column2:
Answer: C. proximal AV fistula, distal AV fistula

Feedback:
Congestive heart failure is more commonly seen with a proximal AVF, while distal ischemia is more commonly seen with distal
AVF.

column1:
Which of the following treatments would not be recommended for chronic peripheral arterial disease?

A: Coumadin therapy
B: Angioplasty
C: Bypass graft
D: Thrombolytic therapy
column2:
Answer: D. Thrombolytic therapy

Feedback:
Thrombolytic therapy is used to treat DVT or acute arterial occlusion by thrombus.

column1:
A 30yr old male smoker is referred for an arterial evaluation for pain and pallor of both forearms and hands. The bilateral radial and ulnar arteries demonstrate diffuse, homogeneous intimal thickening and monophasic flow. The arterial lumen characteristics and flow in the upper arms and shoulders are normal. These findings are most consistent with:

A: Buerger Disease
B: Medial calcification
C: Atherosclerosis causing tunnel stenosis
D: Takayasu Arteritis
column2:
Answer: A. Buerger Disease

Feedback:
The patient most likely has Buerger disease which refers to arteritis of the smaller arteries that is usually associated with males
who are heavy smokers. Takayasu Arteritis normally affects the aorta, subclavian and renal arteries.

column1:
The groin images displayed are from a lower extremity vascular ultrasound. What is demonstrated on the images?

A: Iliac artery dissection
B: Floating strand of thrombus from recanalized DVT in superficial femoral vein
C: Normal common femoral artery bifurcation
D: Floating strand of thrombus from recanalized DVT in iliac vein
column2:
Answer: A. Iliac artery dissection

Feedback:
Note the intimal and media layers of the vessel indicating an artery. The bony structure posteriorly indicates the image is taken
slightly above the groin near the hip joint. An iliac artery dissection is demonstrated. The common femoral artery bifurcation
would demonstrate 2 vessels in the opposite position than displayed on the image. The artery bifurcates as it moves inferiorly in
the leg.

column1:
A patient complains of right leg pain and color changes in the leg. When he lies down, the entire right leg become pale. When he stands up, the entire right leg becomes reddened. The left leg is asymptomatic. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: deep venous reflux
B: significant aortic obstruction
C: right popliteal stenosis
D: significant right iliac stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. significant right iliac stenosis

Feedback:
Pallor and dependent rubor are indications of significant arterial disease. The unilateral presentation indicates the obstruction
is blocking flow to just the right leg. The entire leg experiences the color change, so the blockage must be at the groin = right
iliac.

column1:
Thoracic outlet syndrome occurs when the ________________ is compressed by the _______________.

A: brachial plexus, scalene muscle or cervical rib
B: superior mesenteric artery, median arcuate ligament of the diaphragm
C: renal artery, diaphragm crura
D: celiac axis, median arcuate ligament of the diaphragm
column2:
Answer: A. brachial plexus, scalene muscle or cervical rib

Feedback:
Thoracic outlet syndrome occurs when the brachial plexus is compressed by the scalene muscle or cervical rib. Because the
nerves are also compressed with the lymph and blood vessels, numbness and tingling are common symptoms of TOS.

column1:
A patient with aortic coarctation distal to the left subclavian artery origin will demonstrate:

A: increased bilateral ABIs
B: systemic HTN with increased brachial pressures
C: no change in ankle pressures with exercise
D: bounding pedal pulses
column2:
Answer: B. systemic HTN with increased brachial pressures

Feedback:
Coarctation refers to congenital narrowing of the aorta. It most commonly occurs distal to the origin of the left subclavian
artery. It causes decreased flow distal to the obstruction and monophasic flow with continuous flow in diastole is identified in
the abdominal aorta. Coarctation causes lower extremity ischemia, decreased bilateral pedal pulses, and decreased bilateral
ABIs. Because the arch is proximal to the obstruction, flow to the head and neck is usually normal. Systemic HTN is usually
present because the coarct causes renal ischemia and the renin-angiotensin system is activated causing increased systemic
pressure.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the signs and symptoms of lower extremity arterial disease?

A: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor with dependency.
B: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to rubor with elevation.
C: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to cyanosis upon elevation.
D: Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor upon elevation and rubor with dependency.
column2:
Answer: D. Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor upon elevation and rubor with dependency.

Feedback:
Significant ischemic disease in the leg will lead to pallor upon elevation and rubor with dependency.
Pallor = white, when the legs are raised, blood flow cannot reach the lower legs due to the significant disease in the arteries, the
calf color change is white or pale.
Rubor = red, when the legs are placed in a dependent position, they initially turn a shade of red due to damage to the capillaries
with peripheral arterial disease, the color will change if the legs are raised again.

column1:
Which of the following abnormalities would be best evaluated by asking the patient to hyperextend the leg and point the toes?

A: Popliteal entrapment
B: Raynaud syndrome
C: May Thurner syndrome
D: Paget-Schroetter syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. Popliteal entrapment

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment occurs when the artery is compressed with contraction of the calf muscle. When the patient hyperextends
the leg and point the toes, the calf muscle will contract. PPG or PW Doppler can be used to assess changes in flow during the
maneuver.

column1:
Which of the following describes a positive result from the Adson maneuver?

A: loss of the radial artery pulse
B: loss of pedal pulses
C: pain in the foot with quick dorsiflexion
D: increased hydrostatic pressure
column2:
Answer: A. loss of the radial artery pulse

Feedback:
The Adson maneuver is a patient position used to evaluate thoracic outlet syndrome. If the radial pulse diminishes or
disappears completely during the maneuver, TOS is diagnosed.

column1:
If chronic occlusion is suspected and the artery is not easily identifiable because of atrophy, what is the best way to locate the artery of interest?

A: use color Doppler to locate flow in the adjacent paired vein
B: use PW Doppler to search the surrounding tissues for an arterial waveform
C: use Power Doppler to locate areas of perfusion of the muscles and trace that flow back to the main artery
D: use the PPG sensor and move it around the area of interest to locate an arterial pulse
column2:
Answer: A. use color Doppler to locate flow in the adjacent paired vein

Feedback:
If chronic occlusion is suspected and the artery is not easily identifiable, use color Doppler to locate flow in the adjacent vein.
Most arteries in the extremities have an accompanying vein. A calcified, atrophied artery can be identified coursing adjacent to
its paired vein.

column1:
A patient presents with a diminished pulse in the right radial artery and a normal pulse in the ulnar artery. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: right subclavian artery stenosis
B: proximal right radial artery stenosis
C: right brachial artery stenosis
D: innominate artery stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. proximal right radial artery stenosis

Feedback:
Reduced flow in a single forearm artery would indicate a stenosis in the proximal segment of the affected artery. The other
choices would reduce flow to both forearm arteries.

column1:
Which of the following arteries are evaluated during a penile duplex exam for erectile dysfunction?

A: urethral
B: dorsal
C: ventral
D: cavernosal
column2:
Answer: D. cavernosal

Feedback:
A penile duplex exam can be performed to evaluate a vascular cause of erectile dysfunction. The cavernosal artery diameters
and velocities are evaluated before and after an injection of vasoactive substance. The dorsal vein is also evaluated.

column1:
What arteries are most commonly affected by Takayasu arteritis?

A: calf
B: forearm
C: subclavian and common carotid
D: subclavian and brachial
column2:
Answer: C. subclavian and common carotid

Feedback:
Takayasu Arteritis causes hypertrophic area(s) of inflamed tissue usually found in the aorta and its branches. It is associated
with supravalvular stenosis and aortic coarctation. 90% cases involve the subclavian arteries and over half of the cases involve
the common carotid arteries. It can also affect the renal arteries. Most commonly seen in young females (20-40yrs) and more
common in Asia and India than the US. Symptoms include absence of peripheral pulse, asymmetric brachial blood pressures,
amaurosis fugax, hemiparesis, diplopia, vertigo and upper extremity claudication.

column1:
What causes thoracic outlet syndrome?

A: Viral infection of the intimal layer of the arteries in the upper extremity
B: Medial calcification of the arteries of the upper extremity
C: Extrinsic compression of the subclavian artery from bony and muscular structures
D: Focal inflammation of the proximal subclavian artery
column2:
Answer: C. Extrinsic compression of the subclavian artery from bony and muscular structures

Feedback:
TOS is caused by extrinsic compression of the subclavian artery from bony and muscular structures. The compression causes
ischemia and symptoms in the affected arm/hand. Changes in flow will be detected with patient arm movement.

column1:
Which of the following symptoms is the most commonly seen with peripheral arterial disease?

A: Dry ulcerations
B: Absent pedal pulses
C: Claudication
D: Phlegmasia dolens
column2:
Answer: C. Claudication

Feedback:
Claudication is defined as pain in muscles that subsides w/ rest. The pain will be predictable and reproducible at the same level
of exercise. EX: Calf cramping occur every time the patient walks for 5 minutes

column1:
Which of the following describe a transcutaneous oximetry exam of the lower extremity?

A: Readings are taken in the toes, lower leg and thigh
B: The electrodes cool the tissues to 25 degrees Celsius to release oxygen.
C: A baseline reading is obtained first on the toes
D: It takes approximately 3 minutes to obtain a reading
column2:
Answer: A. Readings are taken in the toes, lower leg and thigh

Feedback:
Transcutaneous Oximetry determines capability of healing of a wound or specific site for amputation. The sensor evaluates
oxygen supply and consumption in tissues surrounding the wound. Electrodes heat the skin to 45 degrees Celsius and oxygen
escapes from the tissue to be measured by the sensor. It takes approximately 15-20 minutes to obtain a reading. System
calibration is REQUIRED!!! First, obtain a reading on the chest as a baseline, then obtain readings of the toes, lower leg and
thigh. Normal TcPO2 levels 70-80mmHg; <40mmHg indicates tissue hypoxia

column1:
Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels are associated with which of the following?

A: Klippel Trenaunay syndrome
B: Giant cell arteritis
C: Marfan syndrome
D: Fibromuscular dysplasia
column2:
Answer: B. Giant cell arteritis

Feedback:
Giant cell arteritis is associated with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. These are both
inflammatory markers found on serum analysis.

column1:
A patient presents with diminished pulses in the right radial and ulnar arteries. Which of the following could cause this symptom?

A: right deep palmar arch stenosis
B: right brachial artery stenosis
C: right superficial palmar arch stenosis
D: right lateral palmar arch stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. right brachial artery stenosis

Feedback:
Reduced flow in the forearm arteries indicates a proximal stenosis. The palmar arch is in the hand, distal to the radial and ulnar
arteries. Any artery proximal to the arteries with monophasic flow could be obstructed and causing the abnormal flow in the
forearm arteries. This includes the brachial, axillary and subclavian arteries.

column1:
Why would exposure to cold increase symptoms of lower extremity arterial stenosis?

A: increased resistance due to vasoconstriction and blood becomes less viscous with cold exposure
B: increased resistance due to vasoconstriction
C: increased resistance due to vasodilation
D: blood becomes less viscous with cold exposure
column2:
Answer: B. increased resistance due to vasoconstriction

Feedback:
Cold exposure can cause the smaller vessels on the hand/foot to constrict which increases resistance to flow. This effect could
further exacerbate symptoms of ischemia in the extremity.

column1:
What complication of Angiography is identified on the image?

A: pseudoaneurysm and arterial occlusion
B: AV fistula and superficial phlebitis
C: pseudoaneurysm, AV fistula and DVT
D: AV malformation
column2:
Answer: C. pseudoaneurysm, AV fistula and DVT

Feedback:
The thrombosed pseudoaneurysm is on the right side (lateral to the vessels). The arrow points to an AV fistula connecting the
femoral artery and vein. The femoral vein demonstrates partial intraluminal thrombus.

column1:
The common femoral artery velocity is 0.8 m/s and the proximal femoral artery demonstrates atherosclerosis formation causing an increase in the velocity to 2.2 m/s. This indicates what level of stenosis?

A: 25%
B: 50%
C: 75%
D: 80-99%
column2:
Answer: B. 50%

Feedback:
When the velocity between two extremity arterial segments doubles, this indicates at least a 50% stenosis. When the velocity
between two extremity arterial segments quadruples, this indicates at least a 75% stenosis.

column1:
A patient presents with groin pain three days after percutaneous balloon angioplasty of the distal right femoral artery. The image on the left is from the common femoral artery and the image on the right is from the proximal femoral artery. Which of the following explains the findings on the exam?

A: Unsuccessful angioplasty procedure
B: AV fistula in the right CFA/CFV
C: The profunda femoral artery is occluded
D: There is a significant stenosis in the right iliac artery
column2:
Answer: B. AV fistula in the right CFA/CFV

Feedback:
The angiography procedure was performed on the distal femoral artery. The images do not demonstrate that area, so the
success of the procedure cannot be evaluated. Atherosclerotic disease normally causes lower resistance flow in all vessels distal
to a stenosis, not just in the vessel affected. The flow in the CFA is low resistance and monophasic but returns to high resistance
and triphasic in the superficial femoral artery. This is a classic sign of the presence of an AV fistula which reduces the resistance
at the site of the fistula and flow returns to normal more distally.

column1:
__________________ uses a catheter with a rotating head to break up the plaque and suction capabilities to remove it from the artery.

A: An angioplasty procedure
B: An atherectomy procedure
C: An endarterectomy procedure
D: A valvulotome procedure
column2:
Answer: B. An atherectomy procedure

Feedback:
Atherectomy uses a catheter with a rotating head and suction capabilities to break up the plaque and remove it from the artery.
Angioplasty uses a balloon tipped catheter to compress the atheroma and expand the vessel lumen. An endarterectomy refers
to when the carotid artery is lacerated, and the atheroma formation is scraped for removal. A valvulotome procedure is used to
remove valves from inside a vein.

column1:
If there is an acute occlusion of the proximal femoral artery, what will the Doppler waveform look like in the mid femoral artery?

A: varies with collateral formation
B: blunted
C: high resistance
D: absent
column2:
Answer: D. absent

Feedback:
There will be no flow in the mid femoral artery with an acute obstruction because there has not been time for collaterals to
form.

column1:
Which of the following conditions would be treated with streptokinase?

A: 70-90% carotid stenosis
B: Venous insufficiency
C: Phlegmasia cerulea dolens
D: Partial DVT
column2:
Answer: C. Phlegmasia cerulea dolens

Feedback:
Streptokinase is only used to break up a clot in patients with phlegmasia due to risk of emboli formation. The benefits of the
drug outweigh the risk of embolism in phlegmasia because it is a critical finding.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes compartment syndrome?

A: typically occurs below the diaphragm in the chest
B: most commonly occurs due to trauma
C: typically occurs below the diaphragm in the abdomen
D: caused by compression of nerves during normal extremity motion
column2:
Answer: B. most commonly occurs due to trauma

Feedback:
Acute compartment syndrome is an emergency, but chronic compartment syndrome is not an emergency. The acute onset of
compartment syndrome affects the extremities and is usually associated with trauma(broken leg or arm). Increased pressure in
the osteofascial compartment will prevent blood flow in and out of surrounding tissues. This can cause permanent muscle and
nerve damage.

column1:
Which of the following is associated with CREST syndrome?

A: Primary Raynaud disease
B: Secondary Raynaud phenomenon
C: Aortic aneurysm
D: Superficial phlebitis
column2:
Answer: B. Secondary Raynaud phenomenon

Feedback:
In contrast to Raynaud disease, Raynaud phenomenon presents concurrently with CREST syndrome, in which tissue necrosis
results from spastic and occlusive disease. CREST syndrome involves symptoms of generalized calcinosis, Raynaud
phenomenon, esophageal dysfunction, scleroderma, and telangiectasia.

column1:
Which of the following is an expected finding with a positive evaluation for thoracic outlet syndrome?

A: elevated blood pressure on the affected side with Adson's maneuver
B: increased flow velocity in the distal arm on the affected side with arm adduction
C: high amplitude PPG digital tracings on the affected side with arm adduction
D: increased resistance in the proximal subclavian artery on the affected side with arm abduction
column2:
Answer: D. increased resistance in the proximal subclavian artery on the affected side with arm abduction

Feedback:
Due to compression of the distal subclavian with TOS, the flow in the proximal subclavian artery will demonstrate increased
resistance. Below the area of compression, flow will be lower velocity monophasic flow with arm adduction. The PPG tracings of
the digits will be low amplitude with arm adduction. The systemic BP will also decrease with patient maneuvers.

column1:
__________________________________ refers to a collection of mucinous material within the adventitial wall layer of the affected vessel

A: Adventitial cyst
B: Mucinous dissection
C: Vasa vasorum
D: Baker cyst
column2:
Answer: A. Adventitial cyst

Feedback:
Adventitial cysts are a collection of mucinous material within the adventitial wall layer of the affected vessel. The cysts
predominantly form in the walls of the peripheral arteries, with the popliteal artery most commonly affected. It is most common
in young to middle-aged men with no other vascular disorders. Usually presents as multiple anechoic or hypoechoic areas
within the wall. No blood flow is detected in the areas. The cysts can cause narrowing of the vessel lumen and stenosis. The
compression of the artery is described as creating the appearance of a Scimitar knife.

column1:
A 30yr old male patient presents with persistent right calf pain with exercise. The image displayed is from the right popliteal artery with the foot in the neutral and flexed position. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: popliteal entrapment
B: Buerger disease
C: Leriche syndrome
D: anterior compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. popliteal entrapment

Feedback:
Popliteal entrapment is a compression disorder that most commonly occurs with flexion of the foot. Note the normal waveform
disappears completely with flexion of the foot/calf muscle contraction.

column1:
What is the normal value for a transcutaneous oximetry exam?

A: 3.5 (unitless)
B: 70-80mmHg
C: 2.0 (unitless)
D: <40mmHg
column2:
Answer: B. 70-80mmHg

Feedback:
Transcutaneous Oximetry determines capability of healing a wound or specific site for amputation; Evaluates oxygen supply and
consumption in tissues surrounding the wound; Measures the oxygen partial pressure in adjacent areas of a wound; cannot
evaluate the wound itself, only the surrounding tissues; Normal TcPO2 levels 70-80mmHg; <40mmHg indicates tissue hypoxia

column1:
If an abnormal transcutaneous oximetry reading is obtained, what should you do next?

A: move the sensor distally
B: move the sensor proximally
C: report the reading location and end the exam
D: turn up the oxygen level entering the patient's mask
column2:
Answer: B. move the sensor proximally

Feedback:
TCPO2 is performed to assess tissues to determine the necessary level of amputation. The amputation must be done at an area
of tissue that has normal healing capabilities. If an abnormal reading is obtained in transcutaneous oximetry, the sensor should
be moved proximally. Keep moving the sensor proximally until a normal reading is obtained and record the location.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes Buerger disease?

A: commonly associated with aortic dissection
B: collagen disease also known as thromboangiitis obliterans
C: associated with uncontrolled diabetes
D: most commonly presents as multiple painless, wet, oozing ulcers of the feet and ankles
column2:
Answer: B. collagen disease also known as thromboangiitis obliterans

Feedback:
Buerger Disease/Thromboangiitis Obliterans is an occlusive disease of SMALL to MEDIUM size arteries caused by inflammation
of arterial wall and surrounding connective tissue.
It is always associated with heavy smoking.
It is most common in men, age 20-30 years.
Collagen disease, such as lupus, is usually associated with Buerger disease.
The disease first affects the plantar or palmar arteries then moves centrally.
Common symptoms include ischemic rest pain and distal dry, painful ulcers.
Evaluation can be performed by Duplex and PPG techniques.

column1:
At the start of an arterial Duplex exam you identify monophasic flow in the left common femoral artery. What should you evaluate next to explain these findings?

A: stop the exam and start a PPG evaluation instead.
B: stop the exam and start a PVR evaluation instead.
C: evaluate the groin for a mass pressing on the common femoral artery bifurcation
D: evaluate proximal arteries feeding the CFA, such as the aorta and iliac bifurcation
column2:
Answer: D. evaluate proximal arteries feeding the CFA, such as the aorta and iliac bifurcation

Feedback:
Monophasic flow in an extremity artery indicates a proximal obstruction. If the CFA demonstrates monophasic flow, you should
evaluate the vessels proximal to the CFA for stenosis.

column1:
What is the most common indication for an upper extremity arterial Duplex exam?

A: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
B: Evaluate hemodialysis graft performance
C: Raynaud Phenomenon
D: Arterial stenosis due to atherosclerosis
column2:
Answer: B. Evaluate hemodialysis graft performance

Feedback:
Because dialysis is a commonly needed procedure, hemodialysis grafts are common. These grafts do mature and even fail over
time so they require serial evaluations with US. Raynaud Phenomenon and TOS are best evaluated with PVR or PPG techniques.
Atherosclerosis formation is uncommon in the upper extremities.

column1:
Which of the following is an expected finding with Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome?

A: Negative Allen test
B: Compartment syndrome
C: Negative Tinel sign
D: Ulnar artery stenosis or occlusion
column2:
Answer: D. Ulnar artery stenosis or occlusion

Feedback:
Hypothenar Hammer Syndrome can be caused by repetitive compression or blunt trauma. The ulnar artery is commonly
affected because it crosses over the hook of hamate bone. It is associated with Raynaud syndrome and most commonly causes
ischemia of fourth and fifth fingers.
Positive Tinel sign = distal paresthesia induced by compression of the affected portion of the entrapped ulnar nerve
Positive Allen test = demonstrates occlusion of the distal ulnar artery
Ultrasound may also demonstrate irregular contour or aneurysm of the ulnar artery .

column1:
This patient has the greatest risk of what complication?

A: rupture
B: DVT
C: dissection
D: distal embolism
column2:
Answer: D. distal embolism

Feedback:
Distal embolization is the most common complication of peripheral arterial aneurysms. A popliteal aneurysm is diagnosed when
the vessel diameter exceeds 1cm.

column1:
Hypothenar hammer syndrome and thoracic outlet syndrome are what type of vascular disorder?

A: Compression syndromes
B: Autoimmune diseases
C: Atherosclerotic diseases
D: Fibromuscular syndromes
column2:
Answer: A. Compression syndromes

Feedback:
TOS involves compression of the vessels in the thoracic outlet. Hypothenar hammer syndrome involves compression of the
ulnar artery. Eagle syndrome involves compression of the carotid artery. SMA compression syndrome can affect the left renal
vein or the duodenum. Median arcuate ligament syndrome involves compression of the celiac artery. There are many issues
that can be caused by anatomic impingement of adjacent structures.

column1: Where does the first caliper go when measuring the acceleration time on PW Doppler? Use your mouse to position the cursor over the correct part of the 3rd waveform and click to set your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Acceleration time is measured from the start of systole to the peak systolic velocity. Prolonged acceleration time can indicate
stenosis proximal to the location where the waveform was obtained.

column1:
A 72yr old female presents for an upper extremity arterial Doppler exam due to asymmetric brachial pressures. The right axillary artery demonstrates a circumferential halo of thickened vessel wall tissue and increased wall vascularity. These findings are most consistent with:

A: Kawasaki disease
B: Buerger disease
C: giant cell arteritis
D: temporal arteritis
column2:
Answer: C. giant cell arteritis

Feedback:
Giant Cell Arteritis usually presents in the cranial arteries(temporal arteritis), but can be seen in the extremities. It is usually
seen in patients 70 - 80 years of age and is more common in women. Associated with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate
(how quickly the red blood cells fall to the bottom of the blood sample) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Asymmetric brachial
blood pressures are seen in patients with arteritis in extremities. Arteritis causes diffuse or focal thickening of the muscle layer
with increased vascularity in the wall of the inflamed segment. Halo Sign - a ring of thickened vessel wall tissue surrounding the
vessel. Doppler findings similar to stenosis.
Temporal arteritis is associated with headaches and tenderness over the artery as is courses extracranially. Buerger disease
affects the small to medium arteries of the extremity. It is most commonly seen in males 20-30yrs of age that are heavy
smokers.

column1:
If the external iliac artery is occluded, what vessels provide the potential for collateral flow to the extremity?

A: genicular arteries
B: medial and lateral plantar arteries
C: inferior epigastric artery and deep circumflex artery
D: internal thoracic artery and lumbosacral artery
column2:
Answer: C. inferior epigastric artery and deep circumflex artery

Feedback:
Lateral branches of the EIA include the inferior epigastric artery and deep circumflex artery that supply the abdominal wall
muscles/skin. They also provide potential for collateralization with obstruction of the EIA

column1:
Which of the following vessels are evaluated in a Doppler evaluation for erectile dysfunction?

A: common femoral artery
B: cavernous arteries and dorsal veins
C: cavernous veins and dorsal arteries
D: internal pudendal artery
column2:
Answer: B. cavernous arteries and dorsal veins

Feedback:
Doppler evaluation of erectile dysfunction includes assessment of the cavernous arteries and dorsal veins with Doppler pre-
and post- medication injection (papaverine most common); Normally the PSV and EDV of the arteries will increase post injection
but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged; PSV >30cm/s considered normal

column1:
The displayed abnormality is most consistent with:

A: Soft plaque formation
B: Thrombus formation
C: Embolism formation
D: Tumor invasion
column2:
Answer: B. Thrombus formation

Feedback:
Plaque forms in arterial vessels. An embolism is a piece of moving thrombus. Tumor invasion would disrupt the integrity of the
venous wall layers. The image demonstrates thrombus adhered to the lining of the vessel wall.
